narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which evaluates the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 68.8% chance that you would not pass, tipping the scales against admission. This prediction drew heavily from your academic record, particularly your Year 3 school decile ranking of 3, which stood out as the strongest factor pushing the model toward forecasting failure—it's notably lower than the average of about 6 for students who went on to pass the bar. Your Year 1 decile of 4 played a similar role as the second-most influential factor, again signaling to the model a performance level below what typically leads to bar success. Coming from an upper-middle income family aligned you closely with many who pass, but this factor still nudged the prediction slightly toward failure compared to lower-income backgrounds that sometimes show unexpected resilience in the data. Your LSAT score of 36 and undergraduate GPA of 3.20 were close to the averages for bar passers—37.27 and 3.24, respectively—but couldn't offset the weight of those decile rankings in the model's eyes. Being full-time, like over 93% of successful students, was a neutral or mildly positive signal that didn't sway the outcome much. Overall, your profile compares to accepted applicants by having solid but not standout test scores and background, yet your mid-school rankings fell short of the higher deciles that dominate among those predicted to pass. The model prioritizes these patterns because past data shows students in top deciles consistently outperform on the bar. While this is disappointing, especially given your dedication as a full-time aspiring lawyer, it doesn't define your potential—many overcome similar hurdles. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to push toward 38 or higher, bolstering your recent grades through additional coursework or a post-bac program to improve those decile standings, and reapplying next cycle with a narrative highlighting your growth. You might also explore bridge programs or schools with more holistic reviews that weigh personal drive alongside metrics. Keep pursuing this path; with targeted improvements, the odds can shift in your favor.
